## з¬¬ 2 йЎө
Chapter 2 The Origin of English Words
1.2 The Indo-European Language Family
It is assumed that the world has nearly 3,000 languages, which can be grouped into roughly
300 language families on the basis of similarities in their basic word stock and grammar. Chinese
is a member in the Sino-Tibetan Family, while English is a member in the Indo-European Family.
The Indo-European is made up of most of the languages of Europe, the near East, and India.
Linguists have divided the Indo-European languages into several groupsпјҲиҜӯж—Ҹпјү, and then
subdivided the different groups into branchesпјҲиҜӯж”Ҝпјү.
The Indo-European languages accordingly fall into an Eastern set: Balto-SlavicпјҲжіўзҪ—зҡ„вҖ”ж–Ҝ
жӢүеӨ«иҜӯж—Ҹпјү, Indo-IranianпјҲеҚ°еәҰвҖ”дјҠжң—иҜӯж—Ҹпјү, Armenian and Albania; a Western set: Celtic, Italic,
Germanic, HellenicпјҲеҸӨеёҢи…ҠиҜӯж—Ҹпјү, Hittite and Tocharian.
In the Eastern set, Armenian and Albania are the sole modern language in the two respective
families. The Balto-Slavic comprises such modern languages as Russian, Prussian, Lithuanian,
Polish, Czech, Bulgarian and Slovenian. In the Indo-Iranian are found Persian, Bengali, Hindi,
Romany.
In the Western set, Hittie and Tocharian find no descendants of their own and Greek is the
modern language derived from Hellenic. In the Celtic, we find Scottish, Irish, Welsh, Breton,
Cornish and Pictish. The Latin language and the five Romance languages, namely, Portuguese,
Spanish, French, Italian and Roumanian, all belong to the Italic. Finally comes the Germanic
family, which is our chief concern as English and its nearest relations are all members of this
family. First, we have the four Northern Germanic: Norwegian, Icelandic, Danish and Swedish,
which are generally known as Scandinavian languages. Then come the four Western Germanic,
German, Dutch, Flemish and English.

## з¬¬ 3 йЎө
Chapter 2 The Origin of English Words
2. A Historical Overview of the English Vocabulary
2.1 Old English Period (450-1066): also called the Anglo-Saxon Period
2.1.1 Historical Development
The First Inhabitants: Celts, also called Britons are believed to be the first people who inhabited
the land that was later to become England; Their language вҖ” Celtic is still another branch of the
Indo-European language family.
The Roman Invasion: In 55вҖ”54 B.C. the Romans led by Julius Caesar the Great invaded the
British Isles, and then occupied the land from A.D. 43 until 410. The Latin of the Roman Legions
became the second major language known in England.
The Invasion of Anglo-Saxons: The withdrawal of Roman troops invited the invasion by the
Picts and Scots from the North. The Celts appealed to Germanic warriors for assistance in
defending their land. Soon the Germanic tribes, called Angles, Saxons and Jutes poured in great
numbers. The new allies of the Celts became their conquerors, and took permanent control of
England. Their language, historically known as Anglo-Saxon, dominated and almost totally
blotted out the Celtic. It is at this time (about 450 AD) when the English language began.
2.1.2 Two events in the Old English Period
1) The introduction of Christianity: In the 6th century, Latin-speaking Roman missionariescame
to spread Christianity in Britain. The introduction of Christianity had a great impact on the English
vocabulary.
It brought many new ideas and customs and also many religious terms from Latin.
2) The Northman Invasion (the 9th century): the land was invaded again by Norwegian and
Danish Vikings. They came first to plunder, then to conquer. With the invaders, many
Scandinavian words came into English.
2.1.3 Characteristics of Old English
вҖў In Old English period, there is a frequent use of coinages known as вҖҳkenningsвҖҷ, which
refers to vivid figurative descriptions often involving compounds.
вҖў The absence of a wide-ranging vocabulary of loanwords force people to rely more on
word-formation processes based on native elements.
вҖў Old English was a highly inflected language just like modern German. Nouns, pronouns,
adjectives, verbs and adverbs have complex endings or vowel changes, or both.
вҖў Old English is believed to contain about 24,000 different lexical items. However, old
English differs greatly from the language we use today.
3 By Min Chen

## з¬¬ 4 йЎө
Chapter 2 The Origin of English Words
2.2 The Middle English Period (1066-1500):
2.2.1 The Norman Conquest:
пҒ· The Normans invaded England from France in 1066.
The Norman French imposed a new political and cultural life on the land of Anglo-Saxon,
and changed greatly the development of the English language. The Norman Conquest virtually
introduced French-English bilingualism into England. The situation of the simultaneous existence
of three languages (French-English-Latin) continued for over a century.
пҒ· The Old English began to undergo much change when the Norman Conquest started a
continual flow of French words into English. The Norman dialect of French became the language
of the upper class, while English completely lost its scholarly and literary importance.
пҒ· By the end of the 11th century, almost all of the people who held political or social power
and many of those in powerful church positions were of Norman French origin. This resulted in a
massive borrowing of French words into English vocabulary.
пҒ· By the end of the 13th century, English gradually came back into the schools, the law courts,
and government and regained social status thanks to Wycliffe translation of the Bible and the
writings of Chaucer.
2.2.2 Characteristics of Middle English
пҒ· The development was the steady erosion of the Old English inflectional systems. Middle
English retained much fewer inflections, with a standard word order making up for their loss. If
we say old English was a language of full endings, Middle English was one of leveled endings.
пҒ· A lot of French words poured into English, covering every aspect of culture and society.
пҒ· Latin words continued to make their way into the English language.
[Sum-up]:
пҒ· In Middle English period, English grammar and vocabulary changed greatly. In grammar,
English had changed from a highly inflected language to an analytic language. In vocabulary
English was characterized by the loss of a large part of the Old English word-stock and the
addition of thousands of words from French and Latin. еҰӮжһңиҜҙеҸӨиӢұиҜӯдёҺе…¶д»–ж—ҘиҖіжӣјиҜӯж—Ҹзҡ„иҜӯиЁҖ

The increase in foreign borrowings is the most distinctive feature of the Renaissance English.

Two most important influences: the works of William Shakespeare (1564-1616) and the King

Characteristics of Early Modern English
In Early Modern English period, English vocabulary grew very fast through extensive
borrowing and expansion of word-formation patterns.
And there was a great many semantic changes, as old words acquire new meanings.
2.4 The Modern English Period (1800-Now):
The Industrial Revolution: With the growth of colonization, British tentacles began stretching
out to every corner of the globe. English began to absorb words from all major languages of the
world. The territorial expansion of the English Empire resulted in the expansion of English words.
Great advances in science and technology: Since the beginning of the last century, esp., after
World War II, the world has seen breathtaking advances in science and technology. As a result,
thousands of new words have been created to express new ideas, inventions, and scientific
achievements.
In Modern English, inflectional word endings were mostly lost. Modern English is
characterized with three main features:
1) The unprecedented growth of scientific vocabulary;
After World War II, neologisms (new words or new meanings for established words)пјҲж–°иҜҚиҜӯпјү
swept in at any rate much faster than that of the pre-war period.
The development of science, the rapid changes in society, the receptive and flexible nature of
English with regard to the influence of other cultures and languages---all these have resulted in a
dramatic increase in vocabulary, a growth which in turn contributes to the richness and
resourcefulness of the English language.
2) The assertion of American English as a dominant variety of the language;
3) The emergence of other varieties known as вҖҳNew EnglishesвҖҷ.

пҒ· ReceptivityпјҲжҺҘеҸ—жҖ§пјү, AdaptabilityпјҲйҖӮеә”жҖ§пјүand HeterogeneityпјҲеӨҡиҙЁжҖ§пјү.
пҒ· Simplicity of Inflection (the progressive decay of inflections has become one of the
developments that mark the evolution of English in its various stages):
Old English: full endings; Middle English: leveled endings; Modern English: lost endings
In Old English, which was a synthetic language, the grammatical relationships of words in
sentences were indicated by the changes of word endings, which are now reduced to minimum.
пҒ· Relatively Fixed Word-order: In the inflected language, the arrangement of words is fairly
free, as the inflections show clearly the proper relationship in the sentence, and ambiguity is
unlikely. But in an analytic language like Modern English, the word-order is required to be
relatively fixed. The semantic relation is closely connected to the positions of the words.
The change of word order may result in a change of meaning.

Difference between American English and British English:
While there are certainly many more varieties of English, American English and British
English are the two varieties that are taught in most ESL/EFL programs. Generally, it is agreed
that no one version is "correct" however, there are certainly preferences in use. The three major
differences between American and British English are:
пӮ· Pronunciation - differences in both vowel and consonants, as well as stress and intonation
пӮ· Vocabulary - differences in nouns and verbs, especially phrasal verb usage
пӮ· Spelling - differences are generally found in certain prefix and suffix forms
The most important rule of thumb is to try to be consistent in your usage. If you decide that
you want to use American English spellings then be consistent in your spelling (i.e. The color of
the orange is also its flavour - color is American spelling and flavour is British), this is of course
not always easy - or possible. The following guide is meant to point out the principal differences
between these two varieties of English.
a. Use of the Present Perfect
In British English the present perfect is used to express an action that has occurred in the
recent past that has an effect on the present moment. For example:
I've lost my key. Can you help me look for it?
In American English the following is also possible:
I lost my key. Can you help me look for it?
In British English the above would be considered incorrect. However, both forms are
generally accepted in standard American English. Other differences involving the use of the
present perfect in British English and simple past in American English include already, just and
yet.
British English:
I've just had lunch I've already seen that film Have you finished your homework yet?

b. Possession
There are two forms to express possession in English. Have or Have got
Do you have a car? Have you got a car?
He hasn't got any friends. He doesn't have any friends.
She has a beautiful new home. She's got a beautiful new home.
While both forms are correct (and accepted in both British and American English), have got (have
you got, he hasn't got, etc.) is generally the preferred form in British English while most speakers
of American English employ the have (do you have, he doesn't have etc.)
c. The Verb Get
The past participle of the verb get is gotten in American English. Example He's gotten much better
at playing tennis. British English - He's got much better at playing tennis.
d. Vocabulary
Probably the major differences between British and American English lies in the choice of
vocabulary. Some words mean different things in the two varieties for example:
Mean: (American English - angry, bad humored, British English - not generous, tight fisted)
Rubber: (American English - condom, British English - tool used to erase pencil markings)
There are many more examples (too many for me to list here). If there is a difference in usage,
your dictionary will note the different meanings in its definition of the term. Many vocabulary
items are also used in one form and not in the other. One of the best examples of this is the
terminology used for automobiles.

Words ending in -or (American) -our (British) color, colour, humor, humour, flavor, flavour etc.

British English and standard American English. However, the largest difference is probably that of
the choice of vocabulary and pronunciation.

Chapter 3 Morphological Structure of English
1. Introduction:
пӮ® Language can be defined as вҖңan arbitrary vocal system used for human communicationвҖқ. By
system we mean that the elements in a language are arranged according to certain rules, but not
at random.
пӮ® Language presents itself as a hierarchy in different aspects. Elements at a lower level are thus
combined according to certain rules to form elements at a higher level. As is shown in the
following hierarchical rank scale, language rises from morpheme at the bottom up to the
sentence at the top in terms of lexicography.
2. Morpheme, Lexeme and Morph:
2.1 Morpheme
2.1.1 Is a Word the Most Basic Unit of Meaning?
Words are usually treated as the basic and minimal units of a language to make sentences,
which are combinations of words according to syntactic rules. But is a word the most basic unit of
meaning?
Words are not the basic meaningful elements of a language, since some words contain several
distinct units of meaning. The linguistic term for the most elemental unit of grammatical form is
morpheme. The word is derived from the Greek word morphe, meaning вҖңformвҖқ.
2.1.2 What is Morpheme?
Syntactically, вҖңmorpheme is the ultimate (the most basic/elementary) grammatical constituent,
the smallest meaningful unit of languageвҖқ. In other words, a morpheme is the minimal form of
grammatical analysis.
In view of word-formation, morpheme can be defined as вҖңthe smallest functioning unit in the
composition of words, not divisible or analyzable into smaller formsвҖқ (Crystal 1985). иҜҚзҙ жҳҜжһ„иҜҚ
ж—¶иө·дҪңз”Ёзҡ„жңҖе°ҸеҚ•дҪҚгҖӮ
Morpheme vs. Word:
Morphemes occur in speech only as constituent parts of words. They can not be used
independently, although a word may consist of a single morpheme. Nor are morphemes divisible

Morpheme vs. Syllable:
The word lady can be divided into two syllables (laвҖ§dy), but it consists of just one morpheme,

2.2 Types of Morphemes:
2.2.1 Free Morphemes and Bound Morphemes
Let us see an example. The word reader can be divided into read and вҖ“er. However, read
cannot be split into smaller morphemes. This means that the word read is itself a single morpheme.
To put it simple, a morpheme which can stand alone as a word is called a free morpheme. By
contrast, -er has to combine with other morphemes. So it is a bound morpheme.
Free morphemes: are the morphemes which are independent of other morphemes. That is to
say, a free morpheme need not be attached to other morphemes; it is one that may constitute a word
(free form) by itself. Free morphemes have complete meanings in themselves and can be used as
free grammatical units in sentences.

Bound morphemes: are the morphemes that can not occur as separate words. They are so
named because they are bound (attached) to other morphemes to form words or to perform a
particular grammatical function; they must appear with at least one other morpheme, free or bound.
Unlike free morphemes, they do not have independent semantic meaning; instead, they have
attached meaning or grammatical meaning. дёҚе…·жңүзӢ¬з«Ӣж„Ҹд№үпјҢеҸӘе…·жңүйҷ„еҠ ж„Ҹд№үжҲ–иҜӯжі•ж„Ҹд№үгҖӮ
2.2.2 Derivational Morphemes and Inflectional Morphemes
Derivational morphemes: are morphemes used to derive new words. In English, derivatives
and compounds are all formed by such morphemes. A derivational morpheme can change the
meaning or the word class, or both the meaning and word class of a word; many derivational
morphemes have a specific lexical meaning. It can be both prefixes and suffixes.
Inflectional morphemes: in contrast, are morphemes used to indicate the syntactic
relationships between words and function as grammatical markers.
The inflectional morpheme does not form a new word with a new lexical meaning when it is
added to another word. Nor does it change the word-class of the word to which it is affixed. It just
adds some grammatical information to the word. Inflectional morphemes are confined to suffixes.

2.2.3 Root, Affix, Stem, Base
Alternatively, morphemes may be also divided into roots (or root morphemes) and affixes (or
affixational morphemes).
AffixпјҲиҜҚзјҖпјү:
Affixes are morphemes which only occur when attached to other morphemes. By definition
affixes are bound morphemes.
There are three types of affixes: prefix, suffix and infix. A prefix is an affix attached before a
root, and a suffix after a root. An infix is an affix inserted into the root itself. Infixing is very rare in
English; the only infix that occurs in English morphology is -m- which is inserted before the last
consonant of the root in a few words of Latin origin.
Root:
As defined by Bauer:
вҖңA root is a form which is not further analyzable, either in terms of derivational or inflectional
morphology. It is that part of a word form that remains when all the inflectional and derivational
affixes have been removed. A root is the basic part always present in a lexeme.вҖқ (Bauer 1983:20)
A root is the basic unchangeable part of a word, and covers the main lexical meaning of the
word. That is to say, a root is the part of the word left, whether free or bound, when all the
inflectional and derivational affixes are removed. It carries the main component of meaning in a
word. Roots are, therefore, the cores of English words.
Historically, the root is the earliest form of a word. Roots are either free or bound:
Free and Bound Root:
All morphemes are bound or free. Affixes are bound morphemes. Root morphemes, can be
bound or free.

2.2.4 Content and Grammatical Morphemes
On a semantic and syntactic basis, morphemes can fall into content and grammatical
morphemes.
Content morphemes are lexical morphemes used to derive new words, so also known as
derivational morphemes (free or bound, with lexical content).
Grammatical morphemes function primarily as grammatical markers (inflectional affixes,
and free morphemes such as in, and, do, have, they, while, where, but and that, functional words).

2.4.1 Lexeme
Lexeme is also an important concept in lexicology.
пӮ® Lexeme is a basic unit of lexical meaning, an abstract linguistic unit with different
variants, for example, sing as against sang, sung. иҜҚдҪҚжҢҮдёҖз§ҚиҜӯиЁҖж„Ҹд№үдҪ“зі»дёӯиҜҚжұҮзҡ„еҹә

1) Why it is abstract?
Lexeme is a word in the sense of an item of vocabulary that can be listed in the ideal dictionary.
It is an abstract vocabulary item, and doesnвҖҷt exist in the language use.
2) What does a lexical unit with different variants mean?
Lexeme is an abstract vocabulary item with a common core of meaning, and can be realized by
different word forms. Put it in another way, lexeme is a set of linguistic signs which share the same
lexical meanings but different in their grammatical meanings.
3) What does a linguistic unit mean?
Lexeme exists regardless of any inflectional endings it may have or the number of words it
may contain. Some set expressions would be each considered a single lexeme.

Sense: the relationship between the words or expressions of a single language. It denotes the
relationship inside the language. The sense of an expression is its place in a system of semantic
relationships with other expressions in the language.

Every word that has meaning has sense but not every word has denotation; besides, the

3.2.2 Social or Stylistic MeaningпјҲзӨҫдјҡж„Ҹд№үпјү
Social meaning refers to the kind of meaning a piece of language conveys about the social
circumstance of its use.
From the use of words we can recognize the geographical, social origin of the speaker, or the
social relationship between the speaker and hearer. Besides, words vary in style, usually ranging on
a continuum from casual or colloquial to formal or polite according to the type of communicative
situation.
3.2.3 Affective MeaningпјҲжғ…ж„ҹж„Ҹд№үпјү
Affective meaning expresses the speakerвҖҷs or the writerвҖҷs personal feelings and attitudes.
In English, some words can express the speakersвҖҷ approval or disapproval of the persons or
things he is talking about.
пҒ” Words that show speakersвҖҷ approval are purr words (appreciative term).
пҒ” Words that show speakersвҖҷ disapproval are snarl words (pejorative term).

Words are related to one another in a variety of ways. These relationships have words to
describe them that often end in the bound morpheme вҖ“nym

commonly accepted as the major possible relationships between words.

II. Synonymy
Synonymy refers the exact sameness or close similarity of meaning. Words that are close in
meaning are called synonyms.
Synonyms are traditionally defined as words different in sound and spelling but identical or
similar in meaning.
Why so many synonyms?
English is a language particularly rich in pairs of synonyms. The primary reason for this has to
do with the history of language and especially with the wholesome borrowing from other
languages..
Generally speaking, the native words are the simplest and most ordinary and tends to belong
to the ordinary colloquial language; The Greek or Latin borrowings are the most learned, most
formal and most technical and they tend to be used in the most formal context; French borrowings
stands between the native words and the Latin and Greek borrowings.
It is obvious that words are synonymous or similar in meaning only in a certain sense, not in
all their meanings.

## з¬¬ 2 йЎө
Chapter 6 Sense Relations
Two types of synonymy:
вҖҳstrictвҖҷ or вҖҳabsoluteвҖҷ synonymy and вҖҳlooseвҖҷ or вҖҳrelativeвҖҷ synonymy
пҒ¬ Strict synonyms refer to two words which are identical in meaning in all its aspects. They are
interchangeable in all contexts. The different choice would have no effect on the meaning,
style or connotation.
Strict synonyms are very rare, found only in words with a rather narrow range of senses. Thus
some linguists even argue that strict synonyms do not exist.
Strict synonyms is uneconomical; it creates unnecessary redundancy in a language.
пҒ¬ When we speak of synonymy, we mean вҖҳlooseвҖҷ or вҖҳrelativeвҖҷ synonymy, where we find not
only a significant overlap in meaning between two words, but also some contexts where
they cannot be used interchangeably.
John found/discovered the basketball in the grass.
Maria Curie discovered radium in 1898.
*Maria Curie found radium in 1898.
Difference Among Synonyms
We often take the following things into consideration when we try to find the differences between
synonyms.
Different English dialects
Different stylistic meanings
Different connotative meanings
Different collocative meanings
III. Antonymy
Antonymy is a relationship of вҖҳmeaning oppositionвҖҷ that may hold between two words.
Antonyms can be defined as words which are opposite in meaning.
Major types of antonymsпјҡ
1) Gradable antonyms: gradable antonyms represent a more/less relation. The words can be the
end-points of a continuumпјҲиҝһз»ӯдҪ“пјү. Since they are gradable, they allow comparison.
2) Contradictory or complementary antonyms: These pairs are called contradictory antonyms
because they represent an either/or relation. The assertion of one implies the denial of the other.
They are non-gradable, so they do not allow comparison.
3) Converse antonyms: converse antonyms represent two-way contrasts. They are relational
antonyms (one presupposes the other).

IV. Polysemy
When a word is first coined, it is always the case that it has only one meaning (monosemic).
But in the course of development, the same symbol may be used to express new meanings. The
result is polysemy.
Polysemy shows the economy and efficiency of human languages.
There are two important processes in the development of meaning:
в—ҷ RadiationпјҲиҫҗе°„ејҸпјү
в—ҷ ConcatenationпјҲиҝһй”ҒејҸпјү
V. Homonymy
Homonymy is the relation between two or more words that are spelled or pronounced in the
same way but differ in meaning. Such words are called homonyms.
Homonyms are considered different lexemes, primarily for the reason that they have
unrelated meanings and different origins.
There are several types of homonyms, in terms of the way of sameness:
в—ҷ lie vi. / lie vi. вҖ” perfect homonyms
в—ҷ tear n. / tear vt. вҖ” homographs
в—ҷ coarse / course вҖ” homophones
Differentiation of homonyms from polysemous words:
1) etymology
2) semantic relatedness
VI. Hyponymy
Hyponymy is the sense relationship of inclusion that relates words hierarchically. The
underlying observation is that some words have a more general meaning, while others have a more
specific meaning, though referring to the same entity.
In semantics this relationship is described according to unilateral implicationпјҲеҚ•иҫ№/еҚ•еҗ‘зҡ„
еҢ…и•ҙе…ізі»пјү.
Two special cases:
1) Taxonymy is the relationship in which вҖңX is a kind of YвҖқ (Cruse 1986). Not all hyponyms
are good taxonyms. Focal orientation; distinctive feature
2) Different from the kind-of relation displayed by hyponymy, meronymy involves
part-whole relation between words.

## з¬¬ 1 йЎө
Chapter 8 Meaning and Context
Chapter 8 Meaning and Context
I. What is context?
As most words have more than one meaning, it is often impossible to tell the meaning of a
word until it is used in discourse, either spoken or written. That is where the context comes in.
No matter how many meanings a word may have, there will be no risk of misunderstanding,
when it occurs in a particular context, for meaning lives in context and the context throws light
on meaning.
II. Types of Contexts:
1. Linguistic Context:
In a narrow sense, context refers to words, clauses, sentences in which a word appears, and it
can also extend to a paragraph, a whole chapter or even the entire book. It is called linguistic
context.
Linguistic context can be further divided into lexical context and grammatical context.
2. Extra-linguistic Context:
In a broad sense, context refers to the physical situation where a word is employed. It is called
extra-linguistic context (non-linguistic context), which includes:
1) Situational Context (the actual speech situation):
пғ  the participants (addresser and addressee)
пғ  time and place
2) Context of Setting (the socio-cultural context, etc.)
e.g., Cultural Context: the different image of вҖңdogвҖқ activated in English culture and Chinese
culture
III. Functions of Context:
1) Eliminating Ambiguity
Ambiguity often arises due to polysemy and homonymy when the word is used in inadequate
context.
The full context can narrow down the meaning of a word or structure. With the context
extended, linguistic or sometimes extra-linguistic, no ambiguity or misunderstanding would arise.
2) Indication of Referents жҢҮзӨәжүҖжҢҮеҜ№иұЎ
A large number of deictic words such as now/then, here/there, this/that, are often used to
refer directly to the personal, temporal or locational characteristics of the situation.
Without clear context, the reference can be very confusing.

## з¬¬ 1 йЎө
Chapter 1 Introduction to English Lexicology
I. Introduction
1. What is language?
Language is a system of arbitrary vocal symbols used for human communication. It is a
specific social action and a carrier of information.
2. What is linguistics?
пӮ® Generally speaking, linguistics can be defined as the scientific study of language.
Major branches of linguistics
phonetics (the study of the characteristics of speech sounds), phonology (the study of the sound
patterns of languages), morphology (the study of the form of words), lexicology (the study of the
vocabulary of a given language), syntax (the study of the rules governing the combination of
words into sentences), semantics (the study of the meaning of language), pragmatics (the study of
meaning in context of use) вҖҰвҖҰ
3. What is lexicology?
пӮ® To put it simple, lexicology is a science of words.
пӮ® To be more exact, lexicology is a branch of linguistics concerned with the study of the
vocabulary of a given language. It deals with words, their origin, development, history, structure,
formation, meaning and usage (or application).
II. Lexicology and Words
1. The connection of lexicology with other branches of Linguistics
Morphology, Semantics, Etymology and Lexicography
2. What is a word?
It can be defined as (Jackson and Amvela 2000):
пӮ® A word is an uninterruptible unit of structure consisting of one or more morpheme.
Uninterruptible means that words are 'Internally stable', namely, a word, as a unit of structure
can not be interrupted by other modifier elements.
Or:
A word is a minimal free form of a language that has a given sound, meaning and
syntactic function.
1

## з¬¬ 2 йЎө
And what does vocabulary refer to?
пӮ® The term вҖңvocabularyвҖқ is used in different senses.
пғЁ the total number of the words in a language;
пғЁ all the words used in a particular historical period;
пғЁ all the words of a given dialect, a given book, a given discipline and the words
possessed by an individual person.
3. Major Features of Words
3.1 Arbitrariness
By arbitrariness, we mean there is no logical connection between sounds and meanings; the
symbolic connection is almost always arbitrary.
However, there seems to be some association between sounds and meaning, since language is
not entirely arbitrary though most words can be said to be non-motivated.
For instance, some words which imitate natural sounds and show a close relation of sound
and meaning are phonetically motivatedпјҲиҜӯйҹізҗҶжҚ®пјүknown as onomatopoeiaпјҲжӢҹеЈ°пјүпјӣsome words
are semantically motivatedпјҲиҜӯд№үзҗҶжҚ®пјүbecause of the mental associations based on the conceptual
meaning of the words such as metaphor.
3.2 DualityпјҲдёӨйҮҚжҖ§пјү
By duality is meant вҖңthe property of having two levels of structures, such that units of the
primary level are composed of element of the secondary level and each of the two levels has its
own principles of organizationвҖқ (Lyons, 1982:20).
A word consists of two sets, or two levels of structures, the basic level and the higher level. At
the basic level is a structure of sounds вҖ” phonemes, which are basically meaningless. But the
sounds can be grouped and regrouped into a large number of units of meaning вҖ” morphemes,
which found at a higher level.
3.3 DisplacementпјҲдёҚеҸ—ж—¶з©әйҷҗеҲ¶зҡ„зү№жҖ§пјү
Displacement means that words enable their users to symbolize objects, events and concepts
which are not present (in time and space) at the moment of communication.
In this sense, animal communication systems are under immediate stimulus control, while
human languages are stimulus free.
3.4 Uninterruptibility:
Uninterruptibility suggests the criteria of 'positional mobility' and 'internal stability':
2

## з¬¬ 3 йЎө
1) Words are said to be 'positionally mobile' in the sense that they are not fixed to specific places
in a sentence.
2) Words are said to be 'internally stable', namely, a word, as a unit of structure can not be
interrupted by other modifier elements. Besides, 'internal stability' also refers to the fact that
within words the order of morphemes remains consistent.
Sum-up:
1) WhatвҖҷs the relationship between sound and meaning?
пғЁ There is no logical connection between sounds and meanings; the symbolic
connection is almost always arbitrary.
2) And what should be the relationship between sound and form?
пғЁ Written form = orthographical record of the oral form
пғЁ Naturally, the sound should be consistent with the form, i.e. the written form should
agree with the oral form.
It is only true in Old English. With the development of the language, more and more
differences arose between sound and form. Why?
пӮ® There are more phonemes than letters in English.
The English alphabet was adopted from the Romans, and it does not have a separate
letter to represent each sound in the language so that some letters must work together in
combination. 26 letters вҶ’48 phonemes
пӮ® Some of the differences were created by the early scribesпјҲжҠ„еҶҷе‘ҳпјү.
вҖ“ Before printing: everything was handwritten, so mistakes were made, for
instance, i, u, v, m, w and n look alike, word spelling is free.
вҖ“ To solve the problem, they change u into o: e.g., Sum, cum, wuman, wunder,
munk пғЁ some, come, woman, wonder, monk
вҖ“ They decided that no word should end with u or v, so they add e at the end, so we
get live, have, due, true.
пӮ® Finally comes the borrowing.
вҖ“ English borrowed words as well as the spelling.
вҖ“ The early ones were assimilated, but the later ones do not conform to the rules of
English pronunciation and spelling.
вҖ“ Stimulus (L) kimono (J) blitzkrieg (G)
4. Classification of Words: пјҲиҜҫжң¬P 5пјү
вҖў English words may fall into the basic word stock and non-basic vocabulary by use
3

## з¬¬ 4 йЎө
frequency;
вҖў Into content words and functional words by notionпјҲжҰӮеҝөпјү;
вҖў Into native words and borrowed words by origin.
1) Basic word stock and non-basic vocabularyпјҲеҹәжң¬иҜҚжұҮдёҺйқһеҹәжң¬иҜҚжұҮпјү
The basic word stock is the foundation of the English vocabulary, forms the core of the
language, and thus is used with a very high frequency. Its characteristics:
вҖў All national characterпјҲе…Ёж°‘жҖ§ / е…Ёж°‘йҖҡз”ЁжҖ§пјүпјҡWords of the basic word stock denote the
most common things and phenomena of the world around us, which are indispensable to all the
people who speak the language
вҖў StabilityпјҲзЁіе®ҡжҖ§пјүпјҡAs they denote the commonest things necessary to life, they are likely to
remain unchanged.
вҖў ProductivityпјҲиғҪдә§жҖ§пјүпјҡWords of the basic word stock are most root words or monosyllabic
words. They can be used alone, and can also form new words with other roots and affixes.
вҖў PolysemyпјҲеӨҡд№үжҖ§пјүпјҡWords belonging to the basic word stock often possess more than one
meaning because most of them have undergone semantic changes in the course of use and
become polysemous.
Non-basic vocabulary includes the words not belonging to the common core of the
language.
пӮ® Words technical in sense (terminology)
пӮ® Slang: belongs to the sub-standard language
пӮ® ArchaismsпјҲеҸӨж—§иҜҚиҜӯпјүare words or forms that were once in common use but are now
restricted only to specialized or limited use.
пӮ® NeologismsпјҲж–°иҜҚиҜӯпјүare newly-created words or expressions or words that have taken
on new meanings.
2) Content words and functional words
Words can be divided into the following classes:
пӮ® Functional words: do not have notions of their own (preposition, pronoun, determiner,
conjunction, auxiliary verb);
пӮ® Content words: denote clear notions (noun, adjective, verb, adverb, numeral);
пӮ® A small number of words of unique function: the particle not and the infinitive marker to.
Functional words: restricted, largely unchanging (numerically stable), serving the grammatical
construction of sentences.
Content words: open, large, constantly changing (numerically unstable, with new words constantly
added), carrying the main meaning of sentences.
4

## з¬¬ 5 йЎө
3. Native Words and Borrowed Words
пӮ® Native words are words brought to Britain in the 5th century by the Germanic tribes.
These Anglo-Saxon words:
a) Small in number вҖ” 50,000 to 60, 000
b) But form the mainstream of the basic word stock
c) In structure: Mostly monosyllabic
Thus, they have the characteristics of the basic word stock mentioned before.
пӮ® Words taken over from foreign languages are called borrowed words (loan words or
borrowings). English is a heavy borrower (with 80 percent borrowed from other languages)
They can be divided into four kinds: пјҲж•ҷжқҗ p 13 вҖ“ p 14пјү
пғЁ DenizenпјҲs еҗҢеҢ–иҜҚпјүпјҡwords borrowed early from Latin, Greek, French and Scandinavian.
They are now well assimilated into the English language.
пғЁ AliensпјҲејӮеҢ–иҜҚпјүпјҡwords which have retained their original pronunciation and spelling.
These words are immediately recognizable as foreign in origin.
пғЁ Translation loanпјҲs иҜ‘еҖҹиҜҚпјүпјҡwords and expressions formed from the existing material in
the English language, but modeled on the patterns taken from another language.
пғЁ Semantic loanпјҲs еҖҹд№үиҜҚпјүпјҡWords of this category are not borrowed with reference to the
form, but to the meaning. Semantic borrowings refer to words which have acquired a new
meaning under the influence of other languages.
